title: Daily Eye Care: Simple Habits for Tired Eyes
excerpt: Ayurveda calls the eyes one of the five organs of perception, and the classical texts offer a complete daily routine, from triphala washes to ghee application, to keep them clear, cool, and comfortable even in a screen-heavy life.
body_html:
when your eyes feel done by noon

Most of us know that feeling: it is barely midday, you have been staring at a screen since breakfast, and your eyes feel dry, heavy, and slightly strained. You blink more than usual, the light bothers you a little, and you rub your eyes without thinking. This is not just a modern inconvenience. Ayurveda recognised it centuries ago and built a daily hygiene system specifically for the eyes, called netra kriya, meaning practices for the eyes.

The classical physicians placed the eyes among the panchagyanendriya, the five organs of perception, and gave them special status. Sushruta, writing in the Sushruta Samhita, devoted entire chapters of the Uttaratantra to netra roga (eye diseases) and their prevention, noting that the eyes are the most precious of the sense organs and deserve deliberate daily care (Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra). Ashtanga Hridaya, a later synthesis that many Ayurvedic physicians still use as a practical handbook, repeats and expands this message, recommending specific daily habits as part of dinacharya, the morning routine (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

vision is governed by alochaka pitta, a sub-type of the pitta dosha that resides in the eyes and processes light and form

the ayurvedic view: pitta, vata, and the fire in your eyes

According to Ayurveda, vision is governed by alochaka pitta, a sub-type of the pitta dosha that resides in the eyes and processes light and form. Pitta is naturally warm and sharp, which is why the eyes are one of the first places to show signs of excess pitta: redness, burning, sensitivity to bright light, and a feeling of heat. When vata also increases, dryness and flickering sensations follow. The Ashtanga Hridaya notes that aggravated pitta and vata together are the root of most netra complaints (Ashtanga Hridaya, Nidanasthana).

Modern triggers map neatly onto this framework. Prolonged screen use, late nights, dust, smoke, and irregular meals all aggravate pitta and vata. The classical answer is not to suppress these doshas with medicine first but to support the eyes daily so they stay in balance, using simple foods, washes, and rest practices.

practical daily habits from the classical texts

Triphala eye wash is the most widely cited preventive practice. Triphala, the combination of three fruits (amalaki, bibhitaki, haritaki), is described in the Ashtanga Hridaya as beneficial for the eyes when used as a wash (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). To prepare it: soak one teaspoon of triphala churna (powder) in a cup of clean water overnight, strain it very carefully through a fine cloth until no particles remain, and use the clear liquid to wash open eyes in the morning. This is mild and cooling when prepared properly. Start once or twice a week rather than daily, and if there is any irritation, dilute further or skip it.

The texts also recommend anjana, the application of collyrium or kajal, as a daily practice for eye health, particularly medicated kajal prepared with soothing herbs (Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra). Commercially available kajal varies widely in quality; if you use it, choose a clean, preservative-free formulation. The underlying idea is that a thin protective layer around the eye helps keep out dust and excessive light.

Netra tarpana, a specialised treatment in which warm medicated ghee is pooled around the eyes in a dough dam, appears in both Sushruta Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya as the classical remedy for dryness and fatigue (Ashtanga Hridaya, Chikitsasthana). This is a clinical procedure done by a trained vaidya, not a home practice. However, the everyday equivalent is simpler: a small amount of pure cow ghee applied to the inside corner of each eye at bedtime, with clean fingertips, is a folk adaptation of this principle that many households have used for generations. Do this only with pure, fresh ghee and clean hands.

Beyond these specific practices, the texts advise looking at natural green landscapes, the sky, and running water to rest the eyes; avoiding reading in poor light or while moving; and sleeping at a regular time, since the liver and eyes are linked in Ayurveda through the pitta connection, and late nights directly stress both (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Eating foods that support pitta balance, including amla (Indian gooseberry), cow ghee, leafy greens, and cooling fruits, also feeds the health of alochaka pitta over time (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

when to see a doctor or vaidya

The habits above are general wellness practices for everyday eye fatigue and dryness. They are not a substitute for proper medical assessment. See a qualified ophthalmologist or medical doctor promptly if you have sudden vision changes, significant blurring, floaters or flashes of light, pain in or around the eye, redness that does not settle within a day or two, discharge, or any injury to the eye. These can be signs of conditions, including glaucoma, retinal issues, or infection, that need clinical diagnosis and treatment.

If you want to try panchakarma-based eye therapies such as netra tarpana or specialised anjana, do so only under the supervision of a qualified Ayurvedic physician, not as a self-administered procedure. This article is general information intended to share the Ayurvedic perspective on daily eye care; it is not a diagnosis and does not replace the advice of a healthcare professional.

Try This Today

  • Overnight triphala wash: Soak one teaspoon of triphala churna in a cup of clean water overnight. Strain twice through a fine muslin cloth until completely clear. Use this liquid to rinse open eyes in the morning. Start two or three times a week; dilute more if there is any mild stinging.
  • Bedtime ghee drops: Wash your hands thoroughly. Take a tiny amount of pure cow ghee on a clean fingertip and place it at the inner corner of each eye before sleep. Blink gently to spread. This mimics the classical netra tarpana principle in a gentle everyday form.
  • Screen breaks with a natural view: Every 20 minutes of screen time, look away and let your eyes rest on something natural, a tree, the sky, or a plant, for at least 20 seconds. The Ashtanga Hridaya recommends gazing at pleasant natural objects as a direct rest for the eyes; the modern interval is a practical way to build this habit in.
  • Palming to settle vata: Rub your palms together briskly until warm, then cup them gently over closed eyes without pressing. Hold for a minute. The warmth calms vata-related dryness and the darkness gives alochaka pitta a brief rest.
  • Amla daily: One fresh amla or one teaspoon of amla powder in water each morning supplies natural vitamin C and is described in the classical texts as particularly nourishing for the eyes via its pitta-balancing action (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Ayurveda calls the eyes one of the five organs of perception, and the classical texts offer a complete daily routine, from triphala washes to ghee application, to keep them clear, cool, and comfortable even in a screen-heavy life.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Alochaka pitta governs vision: screen overuse, late nights, and dust all aggravate it, leading to redness, dryness, and fatigue
A carefully strained triphala water wash and a small amount of pure ghee at bedtime are the two simplest classical home habits for tired eyes
Sudden vision changes, pain, flashes, or persistent redness need a doctor, not a home remedy

glossary_terms.definition: Practices or procedures for the eyes; the Ayurvedic system of daily eye hygiene
The sub-dosha of pitta located in the eyes, responsible for the perception of light, colour, and form
Daily routine; the classical schedule of morning and evening self-care practices described in Ayurvedic texts
A therapeutic procedure in which warm medicated ghee is retained around the eyes in a dough ring; used clinically for dryness and fatigue
Collyrium or medicinal eye liner applied to the rim of the eye; a classical daily preventive practice
A combination of three fruits (amalaki, bibhitaki, haritaki) widely used in Ayurveda as a gentle cleanser and rasayana (rejuvenative)

sources: Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (dinacharya and daily regimen chapters)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttaratantra (netra roga chapters on causes and classification of eye disorders)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttaratantra (netra kriyakalpa: eye treatments including netra tarpana)
Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra (netra roga: eye diseases and their prevention)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Netra kriya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Netra kriya is defined as: Practices or procedures for the eyes; the Ayurvedic system of daily eye hygiene

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to overnight triphala wash. Soak one teaspoon of triphala churna in a cup of clean water overnight. Strain twice through a fine muslin cloth until completely clear. Use this liquid to rinse open eyes in the morning. Start two or three times a week; dilute more if there is any mild stinging.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to bedtime ghee drops. Wash your hands thoroughly. Take a tiny amount of pure cow ghee on a clean fingertip and place it at the inner corner of each eye before sleep. Blink gently to spread. This mimics the classical netra tarpana principle in a gentle everyday form.
